title: My home is not my castle: Kaliningrad as an address for Kremlin propaganda. Part 2
content:
In official discourse, Kaliningrad is increasingly portrayed as Russia’s western fortress: resilient, patriotic, and under constant external pressure. Yet propaganda must also compete with people’s everyday experience. Rising prices, logistical disruption, security alerts and the militarisation of public life shape how residents perceive the consequences of Russia’s war. This article examines that tension between official narratives and lived reality.

The weakest point of Kremlin propaganda in Kaliningrad Oblast is the collision between the official picture of the world and the everyday economy. A person may repeat familiar formulas about ‘unfriendly countries’, ‘sanctions pressure’, and the ‘threat from the West’, while at the same time seeing that the ordinary food basket is becoming more expensive, familiar goods periodically disappear, and the region is increasingly dependent on complicated and costly logistics. In Kaliningrad, the war is felt through the price tag.

title: My home is not my castle: Kaliningrad as an address for Kremlin propaganda. Part 1
content:
Kaliningrad Oblast is a Russian semi-exclave on the Baltic Sea, separated from mainland Russia and located between Lithuania and Poland – both members of the EU and NATO. The region itself was created after the Second World War from the northern part of former East Prussia, transferred to the USSR under the Potsdam agreements in 1945. This makes Kaliningrad simultaneously a Russian territory, a former German space, a Baltic region, and Moscow’s military outpost on the border with the European Union.

Unlike most Russian regions, Kaliningrad for a long time lived not simply in a Russian reality, but in a European borderland everyday life. The local border traffic agreement between Poland and Kaliningrad Oblast significantly increased cross-border mobility. Even after it was suspended in 2016, the memory of that openness remained an important part of local identity. For Kaliningraders, Europe was not an abstract ‘hostile West’ from television, but a nearby and recognisable space just a few kilometres away.

title: Why the information space has become a minefield for EU CSDP missions and operations
content:
When Armenian voters renewed Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan’s mandate in the June 2026 parliamentary elections, they did more than choose a government. They chose closer cooperation with the European Union. That choice made them a target.

As Armenia deepened its relations with the EU, it became a frequent target of Russian Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference (FIMI). Attempts to discredit European engagements are a recurring feature relayed across social media platforms, inauthentic news websites, and foreign state-controlled outlets. FIMI has specifically targeted the EU mission in Armenia (EUMA), which was established in 2023 to monitor and support confidence-building in conflict-affected areas in the country. This pattern is telling: modern influence operations do not happen only during war time. They seek to divide partners, distort facts, shape people’s minds, manipulate their emotions, and influence their decisions.
